My idea was to allow us to define several themes in one file.

All files matching with `<RTV_CONFIG_PATH>/colors/*.cfg` are read because the theme id (example: `monokai-extended`) do not necessarily correspond to file name.

The goal is allow to create themes like:
```
[themename]
...

[themename-dark]
...

[themename-light]
...
```
This theme file will be surely name `themename.cfg`, but theme defined in `rtv.cfg` may be `themename-dark`

Probably we can move parameters update functionality from _save() into this method

```
    def update_parameters(self, obj=None, content=None, content_type=None):
         """
         Method that can be used to adjust file parameters.
         """
        parameters = self.object_parameters.copy()

         # setting the content_type in the key object is not enough.
         parameters.update({'ContentType': content_type})
 
         if self.gzip and content_type in self.gzip_content_types:
             parameters.update({'ContentEncoding': 'gzip'})
         elif encoding:
             # If the content already has a particular encoding, set it
             parameters.update({'ContentEncoding': encoding})

         return parameters
```

Two ways to support removing this test case:

1) it is technically incorrect in CORS to return what this test wants to see i.e. the * should not be returned in AC-Allow-Origin when withCredentials is used.  I found this explicitly stated in Monsur Hossain's book CORS in Action and also in a few StackOverflow threads, most [notably here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/19743396/cors-cannot-use-wildcard-in-access-control-allow-origin-when-credentials-flag-i/19744754#19744754)

2) The test right after this eliminated test has the same issue that is resolved in the fix.  The AC-Allow-Credentials header is not a request header, but rather a response header, so it shouldn't be set by the client.  When you delete it, you then have 2 tests that expect contradictory results, so one needs to be eliminated anyway.

Maybe what I really don't like is how `clear` is called when we're in an entirely unknown state. Imagine a subclass of the BTree (ill-advised, but known to happen) that does something interesting in its clear method---maybe records some stats to graphite or something. If `clear` is called by `__setstate__` and during steady state, that subclass will now be broken, when it wasn't before.

- It's not safe for `__setstate__` to call arbitrary methods until after it's done.
- `__setstate__` is already coupled to `__getstate__` and the internal implementation of the object.

Maybe it would be better for `__setstate__` to simply assign to `self._data` *itself* and avoid the call to clear entirely. Then there's no object churn for the common case of `__init__` -> `clear`, and there's no implicit assumptions about the object state in `__setstate__`, it's all explicit. 

I think I like that best of all, so far.

@abinashpanda The problem with `np.fromiter` is that we can't create 2D arrays using it. So, the solution that I could find is to either `chain` our 2D array, use `fromiter` and then reshape it or use structured arrays. With structured arrays we lose indexing functionality because it creates an array of tuples and therefore numpy considers it as a 1D array.

Comparison current method and chain:
```python
%timeit np.array(list(itertools.product(*[range(i) for i in range(2, 10)])))
1 loops, best of 3: 201 ms per loop

%timeit np.fromiter(itertools.chain(*itertools.product(*[range(i) for i in range(2, 10)])), np.int).reshape(np.prod(range(2, 10)), len(range(2, 10)))
10 loops, best of 3: 121 ms per loop

Line #    Mem usage    Increment   Line Contents
================================================
    10   26.410 MiB    0.000 MiB   @profile
    11                             def my_fun2():
    12   26.410 MiB    0.000 MiB       a = itertools.product(*[range(i) for i in range(2, 10)])
    13   65.660 MiB   39.250 MiB       b = list(a)
    14   65.660 MiB    0.000 MiB       c = np.array(a)


Filename: testing.py

Line #    Mem usage    Increment   Line Contents
================================================
     4   23.352 MiB    0.000 MiB   @profile
     5                             def my_fun():
     6   23.352 MiB    0.000 MiB       a = itertools.product(*[range(i) for i in range(2, 10)])
     7   66.180 MiB   42.828 MiB       b = itertools.chain(*a)
     8   26.406 MiB  -39.773 MiB       np.fromiter(b, np.int).reshape(np.prod(range(2, 10)), len(range(2, 10)))
```
So creating a list is slower and using `chain` is using almost the same amount of memory as creating a list but we are losing readability. So, which method do you think we should go with?

I did a little bit of experimentation that made me question our use of array.array.

Specifically: The main thing we get by using array.array is that we no longer copy elements into a list. How would that be different than returning the object we get from `(visatype.ViReal64 * count)()`, which would be of type `<class '__main__.c_double_Array_10'>` rather than `<type 'array.array'>`?

Very little.

* In both cases the data is zeroed out so that's a shame and the advantage of numpy.
* In both cases the object the user gets back can be indexed.
* In both cases the object's size cannot be changed.
* array.array.__str__() is more useful.

Implementation-wise, the data would still be associated with the flag.  My concern was with the idea of requiring everyone to switch to the `flag('foo')` syntax for referring to the flag, now that I think about it more, you would really only need that when accessing the data associated with the flag, so the decorators could still accept plain strings, as could the top-level `is_flag_set()` helper, etc.  So it probably is less confusing to just have the description match the implementation.

But then the `self.all_units.with_flag()` filters don't make as much sense and the syntax would need to be something like `[unit for rel in flag('foo').relations for unit in rel.units]` (or `flag('foo').all_units` for short).  But I'm not super happy with divorcing the relations and units from the interface instance.  There's tension between the idea that units are "stored in" a flag vs the idea that the units are part of interface implementation.

> Again, when initially writing reactive charms, I thought that states were associated with interface objects

Flags (states) were never associated with interface objects.  They were associated with conversations previously and units in the future.  I think the idea of saying "a flag can have data associated with it" which in the case of interface flags would be a list of units (well, relation ID / unit pairs).

> The reason I did this is if there's an existing rewrite in the original block. If there is, this way will have http redirect to https, which then gets whatever rewrite they originally wanted. If we leave it, then we won't stick the redirect in there at all.

I see what you're saying, but I think this is actually a little tricky (actually more so than when I initially reviewed this PR). For example, imagine the user has the following in their server block:
```
if ($host = "example.org") {
    return 301 http://www.example.org$request_uri;
}
```
When we go to add the redirect, we remove these lines and add a redirect to the HTTPS version of the site. Now there is a redirect loop.

I think that while it'd be nice to remove existing redirect directives to automatically handle cases like the one you described, I think doing so is very difficult. Additionally, I think that in the case we're creating a HTTP server block from an HTTPS one, we should refuse to add a redirect if one exists in the HTTPS block to avoid problems like this.

> I'd almost prefer to explicitly copy over things like logs, and say that if it's anything more complicated than that, should they really be letting us put in redirects for them anyway?

As for whether or not to copy directives in general, while I don't have a very strong opinion, I think I'd rather err the other way. In each case, what's the worst that can happen?

If we don't copy directives, we'll remove configuration for things like logs that were already set up. I feel like if we make a list of directives to copy over, we'll constantly be adding to it as new bug reports come in as we can't possibly know about all crazy directives out there.

If we do copy everything over and refuse to add a redirect when one exists in either the HTTP or HTTPS server block, while we leave a lot of unnecessary stuff in their server block, I'm unaware of any possibilities of incorrect behavior from nginx here. It seems like even if we leave things like SSL directives in the block, Nginx just ignores them.
